title: "An endless demand for compute" | Jonathan Ross, founder of Groq
summary: Jonathan Ross, founder of Groq, discusses the design of Groq's LPU (Language Processing Unit) chip optimized for LLM inference, its partnership with NVIDIA, and inference-time performance trade-offs between specialized hardware and GPUs. The conversation covers static scheduling, mixture-of-experts models, and broader implications of cheaper compute through the lens of Jevons Paradox.
writeup: Jonathan Ross, founder of Groq, walks Julia Turc through the design of Groq's LPU (Language Processing Unit), a chip optimized for LLM inference, and how it compares with GPUs on inference-time performance. The discussion covers static scheduling, how mixture-of-experts models factor into hardware choices, and token cost tracking. Ross frames the economics of cheaper compute through Jevons Paradox, arguing demand for compute expands rather than saturates as cost falls, and the conversation also touches on Groq's partnership with NVIDIA.
